caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit and dominated by non-self-service laundry production work.
a: The CLA survey had 377 usable responses and a 6% response rate, and it is not restricted to the LMM band.
a: Owner labor is omitted from the survey payroll ratio and may contain exposed management tasks.
rho: Digital payment adoption is an enabling condition, not a measured AI implementation rate.
rho: Legacy coin-only stores and route contracts may be slower to integrate than modern card-based stores.
e: The frozen n=25 is an ESTIMATE based on a broad-sector margin bridge, not observed laundry-company EBITDA.
e: The industry survey is store-oriented and may underrepresent larger route operators.
e: Ancillary wash-dry-fold revenue can blur the boundary with 812320.
s5: The survey had a 6% response rate and may attract more transaction-oriented owners.
s5: Plans are not completed transactions, and selling one store may not transfer control of a multi-store company.
s5: The survey includes firms below the LMM band.
q: Expected general price increases do not directly measure pass-through of automation savings.
q: Route revenue-share contracts may capture benefits differently from store-level posted pricing.
d5: BLS publishes the projection only at four-digit 812300, not 812310.
d5: Trade-association demand claims are directional and not a constant-quality volume series.
d5: In-unit machines, improved apartment amenities, and household migration can reduce local laundromat demand.
o: This primitive concerns the need for an accountable operator, not whether customers operate machines themselves.
o: Route laundries and retail laundromats face different bypass risks.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Walk-in customers repeatedly buy washer and dryer cycles at posted vend prices, often supplemented by vending and attended services. Route operators receive recurring cycle revenue or contractual revenue-share from equipment placed in apartments, dormitories, or other third-party properties.
lens.excluded_activities: Non-self-service drycleaning and laundry businesses classified in 812320, linen and uniform supply, equipment manufacturers, passive landlords without an operating service, captive internal laundry rooms, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators of coin-, card-, or similarly operated self-service laundry and drycleaning facilities for external customers, plus firms that supply and service self-service laundry equipment in apartments, dormitories, and other third-party premises. Ancillary wash-dry-fold, pickup, delivery, and vending are included when attached to an eligible self-service operation.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment for the broader 812300 Drycleaning and Laundry Services industry, where production occupations are 52.12% and office support 7.08%; that population contains labor-intensive 812320 and 812330 operations unlike self-service 812310. I therefore use BLS only to locate the exposed office, customer-service, management, scheduling, and record work, then use the industry survey's staffing and payroll evidence to bound current work at self-service stores. This adjustment likely raises the exposed share relative to broad 812300 because 812310 has less production labor, but the trade survey's inclusion of wash-dry-fold services may raise labor intensity relative to pure self-service stores.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit and dominated by non-self-service laundry production work.
The CLA survey had 377 usable responses and a 6% response rate, and it is not restricted to the LMM band.
Owner labor is omitted from the survey payroll ratio and may contain exposed management tasks.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from multi-site 812310 store and route operators, separating already-automated payment and monitoring from remaining administrative and physical tasks, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can address customer-message handling, marketing, bookkeeping, staffing and route scheduling, basic troubleshooting, and performance analysis. Most remaining store wages support attendants, cleaning, wash-dry-fold, security, cash handling, or machine service that software alone cannot substitute. The CLA survey found a median four non-owner employees per store and median payroll equal to 20% of gross revenue among stores reporting payroll. [S2] [S3]

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for broader 812300 Drycleaning and Laundry Services from $29.4 billion in 2024 to $34.5 billion in 2034, a 1.6% annual rate; applying that rate for five years gives roughly 1.08. I center 812310 on that proxy because population, rental housing, and recurring clothing-care needs support volume, then widen the range because broad 812300 includes non-self-service commercial laundry and drycleaning with different trends. The broad projection could overstate laundromats if commercial laundry grows faster, or understate them if renter density grows faster than the broader basket.
caveats: BLS publishes the projection only at four-digit 812300, not 812310.
Trade-association demand claims are directional and not a constant-quality volume series.
In-unit machines, improved apartment amenities, and household migration can reduce local laundromat demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year same-store machine-cycle counts and route turns, separated from price, wash-dry-fold, new-store openings, and acquisitions, would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Self-service laundry is a necessity-led, repeat service, and the trade association ties demand to population density and rental housing. BLS's broader real-output projection supports modest growth rather than rapid expansion. [S4] [S5]

caveats: The frozen n=25 is an ESTIMATE based on a broad-sector margin bridge, not observed laundry-company EBITDA.
The industry survey is store-oriented and may underrepresent larger route operators.
Ancillary wash-dry-fold revenue can blur the boundary with 812320.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level linkage of 812310 establishments, route contracts, ownership, normalized EBITDA, and control rights would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is closely aligned with repeat external-customer self-service operations, so most genuinely LMM firms in the frozen count should meet the service lens. Ineligibility remains for passive property arrangements, captive facilities, misclassified ancillary-service firms, non-transferable contracts, or firms whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the band. The CLA survey shows a fragmented store base: 40% of responding organizations owned one facility and only 13% owned at least five, while a typical store's reported 2023 gross revenue was far below LMM scale; eligible LMM firms are therefore likely multi-site or route platforms rather than the typical single store. [S1] [S3]

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $1,392.1M / sectoral output (T30) $6,341.0M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $135,031 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 812310); class <5: 7,247 firms, avg revenue/firm $223,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,912 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,677 firms, avg revenue/firm $515,883 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,736 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 494 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,120,555 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $175,367 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 196 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,563,520 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $557,691 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,934,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,867,796 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $137,598,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,534,101 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 25

caveats: This primitive concerns the need for an accountable operator, not whether customers operate machines themselves.
Route laundries and retail laundromats face different bypass risks.
o.change_evidence: Household panel data tracking laundromat cycles lost to in-unit equipment, captive building laundry, appliance purchases, and pickup-and-delivery alternatives would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Customer self-service is already the delivery model, but it does not eliminate the accountable operator that leases or controls the site, owns and maintains machines, supplies utilities, processes payments, cleans and secures the premises, and resolves failures. Some quantity can migrate to in-unit machines, building-owned captive rooms, pickup services, or home appliance ownership, but software cannot supply the physical wash and dry capacity.

caveats: Expected general price increases do not directly measure pass-through of automation savings.
Route revenue-share contracts may capture benefits differently from store-level posted pricing.
q.change_evidence: Store- and route-level panels of vend prices, customer volume, contract revenue shares, labor cost, and contribution margin before and after workflow automation would materially change retained benefit.
q.rationale: Posted per-cycle pricing is not contractually tied to labor cost, so implemented administrative savings can remain with the operator. Stores also display pricing flexibility: 82% of survey respondents expected washer prices, dryer prices, or both to increase in the next 12 months. Retention is reduced by transparent local prices, lease and revenue-share negotiations, customer sensitivity, payment fees, and pressure to reinvest in cleanliness, uptime, security, staffing, and equipment. [S3]

caveats: Digital payment adoption is an enabling condition, not a measured AI implementation rate.
Legacy coin-only stores and route contracts may be slower to integrate than modern card-based stores.
rho.change_evidence: Feature-level adoption, workflow completion, exception, and labor-hour data from major vended-laundry payment and management platforms across installed stores would materially change this range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible in centralized customer support, marketing, accounting, route optimization, staffing, exception triage, and machine-performance analysis because digital payment and proprietary app infrastructure already exists at many stores. The 2024 survey found 42% accepted credit or debit cards, 25% smartphone wallet payments, and 25% a proprietary mobile app, although 21% remained quarter-only. Integration with old machines, fragmented software, low administrative headcount, security, and hands-on exceptions prevent full implementation. [S3]

s5.bridge: The 2024 CLA survey measures whether an organization planned to sell any self-service laundry in the next 12 months; 17% said yes. I convert that one-year intention into a bounded five-year qualifying-control probability without treating yearly intentions as independent: I add future cohorts but haircut for non-completion, partial store sales that do not transfer control of the eligible firm, repeated plans, and respondents outside the LMM band. The source can overstate qualifying transfers because it asks about any store sale, yet understate a five-year window because it observes only current 12-month plans.
caveats: The survey had a 6% response rate and may attract more transaction-oriented owners.
Plans are not completed transactions, and selling one store may not transfer control of a multi-store company.
The survey includes firms below the LMM band.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year panel linking listed and completed self-service-laundry transactions to firm ownership, EBITDA, and whether control changed would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Industry-specific sale intent is unusually direct evidence of a liquid asset-transfer market, but the screen requires control of an eligible operating firm rather than any individual laundromat sale. The base therefore sits well below a naive fivefold extrapolation of the 17% one-year intention. [S3]

principal_driver: Repeat necessity demand, posted cycle pricing, and industry-specific sale intent support a durable transferable operating model.
principal_weakness: The core service is already self-automated, so the remaining AI-exposed wage pool is narrower than the business's mechanized appearance suggests.
